coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of blue-sparkling N, S-doped carbon dots for effective detection of nitro explosive and Fe3+ ion and anti-counterfeiting studies
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Annamalai, Kumaresan
Ravichandran, Ramya
Annamalai, Arun
Jeevarathinam, Anandhavalli
Suresh, Ranganathan
Elumalai, Sundaravadivel
coredata.dc:description: Highly blue-luminescent nitrogen & sulfur-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) made with homocysteine and ethylene diamine by simple single-step microwave-assisted style. The prepared carbon dots showed a higher photoluminescent quantum yield (PL-QY), which is found to be 45 %. FTIR and XPS analysis reveals that these N, S-CDs contain rich nitrogen, and sulfur as well as C=O and C=C functional groups. N, S-CDs had a mean size around 3-4 nm, very interestingly, obtained carbon dots exhibit excitation-dependent photoluminescent character, are water-soluble, and restore luminescent stability under UV-light exposure and a wide range of pH. The crafted N, S-CDs are hired for precise and discriminating detection of multiple water impurities such as Fe3+ ion and Picric acid. The complexation between oxygen species of N, S doped CDs, and Fe3+ ion follows the inner-filteration effect (IFE) and with Picric acid follows Forster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) for the doped N, S-CDs are found to be the utmost possible mechanism for the observed sensing performance. The lower detection limit (LOD) of Fe3+ sensors is about 0.11 µM and for picric acid is 0.07 µM. Moreover, these blue-emissive N, S-CDs as competent fluorescence sensors, have been used as fluorescent inks, as well as in advanced portable devices information security and filter paper-based probes for visual recognition might also hold prominent ability to make wider applications in biological systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N, S-doped CDs
Picric acid
Fe3+ ion
Energy transfer
Inner-filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of N, S-CDs using microwave-assisted treatment
N, S-CDs a detection probe for picric acid and Fe3+ ion
Zooming the morphological studies and analysis of N, S-doped CDs
Label-free selectivity & sensitivity studies of N, S-doped CDs
Effect of picric acid and Fe3+ ion on fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs
Fluorescence quenching N, S-doped CDs on picric acid
Mechanism for quenching of N, S-CDs – picric acid
Mechanism for quenching of N, S-CDs - Fe3+ion
Recognition of PA and Fe3+ in real world test samples
Filter paper-strip-based probe for visual recognition of toxins, anti-counterfeiting, and ink studies
Comparative study of N, S-CDs with previously reported sensor probes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) 2D HR-TEM images and histogram of average particle size distribution of N,S-CDs (b) Lattice fringes of N,S-CDs, (c) shows the mean particle size histogram of N, S-CDs from DLS measurements, (d) XRD pattern of N,S-CDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of N, S-CDs, (b) C1s spectrum of N, S-CDs, (c) N1s spectrum of N, S-CDs, (d) O1s spectrum of N, S-CDs, (e) S2p spectrum of N, S-CDs, and (f) FT-IR spectrum of N, S-CDs.
(a) Normalized absorption and emission spectrum of N, S-CDs, (b) Emission and excitation spectrum of N, S-CDs and (c) Emission spectrum at various excitation wavelengths of N, S-CDs.
(a) Selectivity of different aromatic nitroexplosive compounds as well as metallic cations, (b) and (c) Sensitive recognition of N,S-CDs in existence of PA as well as Fe3+ ion.

(a) and (d) displays the PL spectrum of quenched fluorescence for N,S-CDs on ascending the concentration of Fe3+, PA ion at 350 nm excitation wavelength, (b) and (e) displays mono-exponential variant of the quenching efficacy of (Io/I) through Fe3+ ion as well as picric acid respectively, (c) and (f) displays the linear variant of (Io/I) through Fe3+ ion as well as picric acid respectively.

(a) and (b) displays the UV absorption-spectral overlap and PL emission of N,S-CDs/Fe3+ and N,S-CDs/picric acid, (c) Graphical illustration of feasible quenching mechanism, (d) and (e) displays average decay lifetime measurements of N,S-CDs with Fe3+ ion as well as PA.

(a) shows N,S-CDs anti-counterfeit studies under 365 nm without and with UV-lamp. (b) & (c) displays fluorescent-ink under 365 nm without and with UV-lamp. (d) & (e) displays fluorescent whatman filter paper-based sensor probe for optical recognition of PA and Fe3+.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of blue-sparkling N, S-doped carbon dots for effective detection of nitro explosive and Fe3+ ion and anti-counterfeiting studies

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, Tamil Nadu 603203, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: SRM Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Chemistry, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur, Tamil Nadu 603203, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Center for Material Chemistry, Karpagam Academy of Higher Education, Coimbatore, Tamil Nadu 641021, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Chemistry, Center for Material Chemistry, Karpagam Academy of Higher Education, Coimbatore, Tamil Nadu 641021, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly blue-luminescent nitrogen & sulfur-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) made with homocysteine and ethylene diamine by simple single-step microwave-assisted style. The prepared carbon dots showed a higher photoluminescent quantum yield (PL-QY), which is found to be 45 %. FTIR and XPS analysis reveals that these N, S-CDs contain rich nitrogen, and sulfur as well as C=O and C=C functional groups. N, S-CDs had a mean size around 3-4 nm, very interestingly, obtained carbon dots exhibit excitation-dependent photoluminescent character, are water-soluble, and restore luminescent stability under UV-light exposure and a wide range of pH. The crafted N, S-CDs are hired for precise and discriminating detection of multiple water impurities such as Fe3+ ion and Picric acid. The complexation between oxygen species of N, S doped CDs, and Fe3+ ion follows the inner-filteration effect (IFE) and with Picric acid follows Forster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) for the doped N, S-CDs are found to be the utmost possible mechanism for the observed sensing performance. The lower detection limit (LOD) of Fe3+ sensors is about 0.11 µM and for picric acid is 0.07 µM. Moreover, these blue-emissive N, S-CDs as competent fluorescence sensors, have been used as fluorescent inks, as well as in advanced portable devices information security and filter paper-based probes for visual recognition might also hold prominent ability to make wider applications in biological systems.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescent carbon (C) quantum dots (CDs) which are less than 10 nm in size have drawn enhancing responses as ecologically accessible nanomaterials, owing to their promising characteristics of lesser toxicity, excellent water dispersity, varied carbon sources availability, and outstanding emission visibility nature [1,2]. (CDs), have attracted extensive approachability from both research academics and industries because of their excellent benefits, such as steady photoluminescent properties, versatile surface interaction, biocompatibility, and strong chemical inertness [3,4]. Due to these aids, CDs were employed in an extensive range of applications such as drug delivery, recognition of toxic metal ions and nitro-explosive compounds, bioimaging, and light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [5–7]. As of now, traditional fluorescent materials containing cadmium (Cd), lead (Pb), gold (Au), silicon (Si) silver (Ag), and gold (Au), have been widely prepared [8]. However, these materials, have increased concerns over ecological hazards, latent toxicity, and unbearable cost [8]. Hence, it is significantly important to improve facile, eco-friendly, and cost-effective techniques for the preparation of fluorescent nanomaterials with outstanding photoluminescence and nominal toxicity.

Usually, bottom-up approaches are generally embraced for the preparation of carbon dots by carbonization of carbonaceous organic matter using methods, such as microwave treatment [1], pyrolysis, ultrasonic-assisted methods [9], and hydrothermal/solvothermal treatment. Between these techniques, microwave-assisted methods have distinctive benefits, such as being fast, facile, dynamic method, and harvesting enhanced product yield [1]. In recent times, molecular and biological carbon precursors, such as carbohydrates, polyhydric acid, strawberry juice, and pomelo peel, were also employed to make CDs as greener approaches [10]. Nevertheless, to prepare fluorescence CDs with satisfactory quantum yields (QYs), a surface-passivating practice is essential [11], or else, by using nitrogen-containing precursors of highly photoluminescence CDs could be made without surface-passivating mediators [10].

Picric acid (PA) is also recognized to be 2,4,6-tri-nitrophenol (TNP), a chief among the nitroexplosives. TNP is primary component towards insect repellent and toxic drugs [12]. Owing to its explosive nature, it is utilized as a burster inland mining. Due to its unique water-soluble nature, TNP can be able to easily pass into living creatures via marine organisms. Intake of TNP even at very small concentrations could make a strong impact on the human body with respiratory problems, tumors, and eye/skin irritation, and also mortal to environmental contamination [13]. Therefore, the permissible concentration level of TNP could be a lesser amount than 2.18 µM specified by “Drinking Water Health Standards of China” (GB5749-2006) and “Surface Water Environmental Quality Standard” (GB3838-2002) [14].

The foremost origin for rapid ecological scarcity is caused by disposed inorganic toxins from industries in environmental aquatic systems [15]. Mostly this is due to prompt growth in industrial development, illicit and inappropriate waste discards, insecticides, and manures [16]. Heavy metal ions in the environment are very harmful to both humans and animals [17]. Iron (Fe3+) is a vital element for humans and plays a significant role such as enzyme catalysis, oxygen metabolism in biological systems, and the electron-transfer process [18]. Yet, the extreme increase and deficiency of Fe3+ from the standard admissible limit could result in harsh health inconveniences such as blood syndrome, organ impairment, and ultimately demise [19]. In the human body, there is 4-5 g of iron exists. The admissible level of Fe3+ ion in potable water was 0.3 µM provides the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [20]. Hence, the recognition of TNP and Fe3+ ion fetches a significant and critical duty for conservationists to scrutinize the existence of toxic metallic ions in a contaminated location.

Inductively-coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) [21], and atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [22] are the conventional analytical methods used for the detection of environmental contaminants. However, these methods have some difficulties such as higher costs, and challenging groundwork. Consequently, there is a significant requirement for technologies to detect nitro-aromatic explosive compounds and heavy metal contaminants from the environment. For recognizing the environmental contaminants in the surroundings, extensive efforts were necessary to increase the cost-effective and selective recognition. Detection by fluorometric method has various advantages including economical, prompt response, and high sensitivity [23]. The enhancement of observing devices such as sensors for the detection of nitro-aromatic explosive compounds and poisonous heavy metal ions is vital to ecological protection and human welfare.

A variety of carbon sources can be available for the preparation of CDs through a microwave reaction, a convenient and efficient agile method. In contrast to natural resources such as grass, plants, and orange juice materials [24,25]; in this work, we focus on amino acid-derived carbon dots. The amino acid L-homocysteine holds not only nitrogen but also contains sulfur atoms in its molecular assembly. These elements play as a self-heteroatom dopant with suitable energy levels to enrich the blue emission intensity [26]; owing to these properties we picked homocysteine as a carbon source. To the best of our knowledge, the synthesis of homocysteine with ethylene diamine precursor derived N, S-CDs using a microwave-assisted approach has not earlier been reported. We used a microwave heating method as this lets the reaction solution to be more rapidly and uniformly heated [1].

In the present work, facile microwave-assisted production of dual heteroatom doped blue-emitting homocysteine and ethylene diamine derived N, S-CDs. The prepared N, S-CDs show outstanding water-solubility with a relatively greater quantum yield of about 45 % which was higher than CDs which was produced from other amino acid precursors in earlier reports. The N, S-CDs synthesized had been utilized for quantitative estimate the Fe3+ and picric acid (PA) detection in water as well as real sample analysis. The metal ions display a straight interaction with functional moieties of CDs, prepared from amino acid precursors [27] causing quenching of fluorescence, used for the quantitative estimation of toxins in aqueous environment. The quenching of CDs intensity by metal ions and nitroexplosive compounds in the presence of N, S-CDs via different quenching mechanisms showed a linear response range of 0–50 µM was found for Fe3+ and PA detection. The N, S-CDs detection probe displays the detection limit (LOD) value of 0.11 µM and 0.07 µM for Fe3+ ions as well as picric acid, individually. Also, the possibility of the detection ability of the sensor probe was checked for metallic cations and nitroexplosive compounds in real water samples collected from nearby zone.

L-Homocysteine, ethylene diamine, Picric acid (trinitrophenol), p-nitroaniline, o-nitrophenol, m-dinitrobenzene, 4-nitrotoluene, o-nitroaniline, 2,4-dinitrophenol, o-nitrophenol, p-nitrophenol, NiCl2.6H2O, Pb(CH3COO)2, FeCl3, Hg(CH3COO)2, CrCl3.6H2O, MnCl2.4H2O, KCl, CoCl2.6H2O, CuCl2.2H2O, NaCl, FeCl2, and AgCl, were purchased from Merck, Sigma Aldrich, Finar, and SRL Private Limited. All chemicals were utilized without undergoing any additional purification. The experiment exclusively employed de-ionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N, S-CDs using microwave-assisted treatment
ce:section.ce:para: 500 mg of L-Homocysteine and 5 mL of ethylene diamine were taken in a 100 ml beaker. The reactant mixture was sonicated for 5 min to yield a clear solution. After that, the mixture was allowed to enter into the 750 W domestic microwave oven (Panasonic) for 10 min to get a brownish-black product obtained. Then the product was brought to room temperature. The obtained product was centrifuged, dissolved with distilled water, and subjected to dialysis for 48 h through a dialysis film (MW 1000) to remove the unreacted residue and then stored for further analysis. The preparation procedure is shown in Fig. 1 .

Microstructural images of N, S-CDs were examined using TECNAI G20 F20 (FEI, USA) high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) operating at 200kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) studies were determined on a D/max-3A diffractor. The Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR) was recorded via (Bruker Alpha) ranging from 4000 cm-1 to 400 cm-1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded by using a uni-X-ray source with Al K α excitation (1486.6 eV) on an ESCALAB 250 spectrometer. Particle size analysis using dynamic light scattering (DLS) and measurement of zeta potential were conducted using a Horiba Scientific SZ-100 instrument. The UV-3600 spectrophotometer from Shimadzu, Japan, was employed to measure optical absorption. Fluorescence spectroscopy was performed using a LS-45 Fluorescent Spectrometer from Perkin Elmer, employing a slit width of 10 nm for both excitation and emission, with a PMT voltage set at 650 V. The shift occurs form 250–500 nm when the excitation was increased by 20 nm. Time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC), i.e. Fluorescence lifetime decay spectral analysis performed with a FLS980 (Edinburgh Instruments). The optical properties had been taken with a quartz cuvette with 1 cm path length.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N, S-CDs a detection probe for picric acid and Fe3+ ion
ce:section.ce:para: We examined the fluorescence response of N, S-doped CDs with different metal cations and nitro-aromatic explosives. A 5 mM stock solution of several nitro-aromatic explosive compounds and toxic metal ions was arranged for sensor studies. The discernment studies of Fe3+ ion and PA detection, for we have verified numerous metal ions and nitro-explosive mixes such as ortho-nitrophenol, dinitrobenzene, para-nitrophenol, dinitrophenol, ortho-nitrotoluene. For the selectivity assessment, para-nitrotoluene, nitro-toluene, picric acid and Fe2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Na+, Fe3+, K+, Hg2+, Ag+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, were added to the N, S-doped CDs solutions independently. The sturdy rising concentrations of Fe3+ cations have been raised gradually towards N, S-CDs at room temperature. A similar condition was used for picric acid detection. The fluorescence quenching technique end to end with excitation of 350 nm wavelength for fluorometric selectivity, as well as sensitive recognition of Fe3+ ion and PA, was prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Zooming the morphological studies and analysis of N, S-doped CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 a displays 2D-TEM images of the homocysteine and ethylene diamine derived N, S-CDs. A mean size of mono-dispersed spherical shape of around 3-4 nm is observed from the TEM images. Moreover, we measured the lattice spacing of the as-synthesized material around 0.32 nm depicted in Fig. 2b apart to exemplify the sample. These N, S-CDs display the mean particle size of almost 4 nm (Fig. 2a inset) was calculated with the help of ImageJ software. Similarly, the DLS measurements illustrated in Fig. 2c were utilized to analyze the distribution of particle sizes for co-doped N, and S-CDs. A hydrodynamic diameter of resultant co-doped N, S-CDs, obtained from a combination of ethylene diamine and homocysteine, was determined to be 4.1 ± 0.8 nm. Subsequently, in XRD (Fig. 2d) the peak is positioned at 23° parallel to the (002) plane and aligns with the presence of an amorphous region, and this observation follows findings reported in previous studies [28]. Thus, the present approach gave a unique size distribution of co-doped N, S-CDs, which is dispersible in a water medium.

To examine the chemical composition elements present in N, S-CDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used. The XPS survey spectra (Fig. 3 a) of N, S-CDs display four evident peaks of around 285 eV for C1s, 399.7 eV for N1s, 533 eV for O1s and a modest S2p peak at nearly 165 eV. The deconvoluted C1s spectra shown in Fig. 3b deconvoluted by three peaks, which were consigned to the C=C bond at nearly 284.7 eV, C–N at 286.5 eV, and C–S at 287.4 eV. Fig. 3c displays the deconvoluted N1s spectra revealing the presence of three nitrogen groups of the C–N bond at 399.1 eV and the N–H group at 400.3 eV, and 398.3 eV. Fig. 3d shows deconvoluted O1s spectra into three peaks ascribed to the presence of C=O group at 530.8 eV, C–O at 531.8 eV, and C–OH group at 530.1 eV. Furthermore, the spectrum Fig. 3e, displayed for sulfur comprises two humps, sited at 164.7 eV and 167.3 eV, signifying the existence of two types of the sulfur bonding. These two peaks ascribed to 2p3/2 as well as 2p1/2, correspond to –C–S– bond (i.e. covalent type) present in homocysteine amino acid, arising from spin-orbit couplings [29].

To inspect the various surface functional groups present in the N, S-CDs surfaces, the FT-IR studies were performed. Fig. 3f shows the FT-IR spectrum for N and S-CDs obtained from a mixture of homocysteine and ethylene diamine. The intense and wide-ranging peak observed at 3200–3400 cm-1 is owing to the presence of –OH stretching from the -COOH group and another sharp peak at 1702 cm-1 might be due to the presence of C=O stretching (strong) from the -COOH group. These functional groups are formed by the condensation reaction between the homocysteine and ethylene diamine. The peak at 1086 cm-1 is due to the C-N stretching vibration. The peak at 1582 cm-1 is due to C-H stretching vibration. The carbonyl stretching and N–H stretching vibrations were evident by the bands from 1510 to 1680 cm−1, while a peak at 3420 cm−1 band was attributed to N–H stretching vibrations. A peak at 2550 to 2600 cm−1 would be attributed to S–H stretching vibrations, and the peak at 678 cm-1 was ascribed to the C-S stretching vibrations of a single bond and proved that sulfur was successfully doped into N and S-CDs. These results demonstrate that obtained N, S-CDs from the mixture of ethylene diamine and homocysteine provide unique functional groups, which assist in dispersing the N, S-doped CDs in water. Additionally, the above FTIR analysis also indicates that the N, and S-CDs have amino groups at their surface, which significantly improves the colloidal stabilities of N, and S-doped c-dots in aqueous solution they might be also composed of amorphous inorganic carbon. The overall XPS deconvoluted spectra and FT-IR reveal that the CDs formed with electron-rich self-doped N, O, and S atoms also with various functional moieties like C=O, C-S, C-OH, amino and C-O, etc. These electron-rich functional groups and heteroatom are the major reason for the higher quantum yield and excitation-dependent emission properties of the N, S-CDs.

To study the optical analysis for the synthesized N, S-CDs, UV–visible spectra and fluorescence spectra were measured. Fig. 4 a, shows the corresponding absorption obtained from homocysteine amino-acid and ethylene diamine. The N, S-CDs synthesized display a minor absorption band at 270 nm, which arises from the π−π* transition of C=C bonds embedded within the N, S-CDs. The second peak observed at 350 nm is associated with the n−π* transition of diverse hetero-atomic functional assemblies present on the N, S-CDs surface. The N, S-CDs obtained from homocysteine and ethylene diamine in Fig. 4a show the highest emission peak and highest intensity is 451 nm (λ ex = 360 nm) demonstrating these N, S-CDs have wonderful light harvesting materials. Fig. 4b, shows the excitation spectrum band at 248 nm and 352 nm. As in Fig. 4c, the N, S-CDs have highly intense emission peaks, which are fully excitation-dependent properties excited from 250 nm to 450 nm. These N, S-CDs showed interesting photoluminescent behavior, it is further motivated us to determine the photoluminescent quantum yield (PL-QY). We determined the PL-QY of obtained C-dots was calculated and it is in the range of 45 %.

The stableness of the prepared N, S-CDs is the chief significance for the wide-ranging applications. To elucidate the multi-functional performance of homocysteine and ethylene diamine derived N, S-CDs, their stabilities were examined with different environmental factors. The photo-stabilities of N, S-doped CDs were confirmed via constant irradiation at 365 nm UV light. The intensities of N, S-doped CDs were not much altered on raising the irradiation time up to 8 h (Fig. S1a). Also, even after two months of storage in ambient conditions, the colour of N, S-CDs solution almost remains not changed (Fig. S1b). These outcomes revealed that, better stabilities for N, S-CDs. (Fig. S1c) illustrates the fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs across different NaCl concentrations. The photoluminescence (PL) intensity of N, S-CDs remains unchanged with varying ionic strength, representing the N,S-CDs aqueous solution, maintains stability even in occurrence of elevated ion concentrations. In addition, pH studies (Fig. S1d) of these N, S-CDs had been examined. As investigation into pH effects revealed a significant influence on the photoluminescence (PL) intensity of the synthesized N, S-CDs. With the pH range of 1–5, the PL intensity of N, S-CDs exhibited enhancement with an increasing pH, reaching its peak at pH 5. Beyond pH 5, a consistent decline in the PL intensity of the prepared N, and S-CDs was observed. The N, and S-CDs exhibit enhanced stability, especially in highly acidic conditions. The pH-responsive characteristics displayed by these N, S-CDs make them promising for potential applications in pH sensor studies. The zeta potential (Fig. S1e) value of this N, S-CDs is + 24.7 mV and indicates the high dispersibility nature of the material. These results showcase the superior stabilities of the fluorescent sensor probe, as well as its enduring colloidal stabilities, underscoring the exceptional stabilities of the synthesized fluorescent material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Label-free selectivity & sensitivity studies of N, S-doped CDs
Effect of picric acid and Fe3+ ion on fluorescence intensity of N, S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In acidic (pH = 5) environments, we introduced several nitroaromatic compounds and toxic metal cations to examine the consequence on fluorescence intensities for as-prepared N, S-CDs on 451 nm as well as their concentrations of about 5 mM. Owing to the occurrence of rich functional assemblies (nitrogen & carbon) on N, S-CDs surface, the nitroaromatic compounds and metallic ions could significantly alter N, S-CDs fluorescence intensity. Amongst the metallic ions and nitro aromatic compounds checked, only Fe3+ ion and picric acid could alter the PL intensity, and other metal cations and nitro explosives displayed no impact on PL intensities of N, S-doped CDs as displayed in Fig. 5 a. The above outcomes exposed that as-synthesized N, S-CDs demonstrates for picric acid as well as Fe3+ recognition in aqueous conditions. Fig. 5a exhibits selective PL quenching efficacy of N, S-CDs against different metal cations and nitro-compounds with a concentration of 200 µM. Whereas intensifying the concentration for picric acid as well as Fe3+ ion, the N, S-CDs fluorescence intensity was quenched. The fluorescence intensity of picric acid as well as Fe3+ was expressively quenched, it designates the selective detection of nitroaromatic compounds as well as metallic ion. The sensitivity of N, S-doped CDs through picric acid as well as Fe3+ ion in the existence of supplementary nitroaromatic explosives as well as metallic ions are shown in Fig. 5b & c.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching N, S-doped CDs on picric acid
ce:section.ce:para:
In our primary studies, the fluorescence quenching of N, S-CDs against the family of aromatic nitro compounds was examined. In physical organics, aromatic nitro compounds are used as typical studies as their physicochemical properties are expressively altered by the aromatic ring substituents. We took N, S-doped CDs (8.2 µg/mL) with different nitro aromatic explosives (0.05 mM). Fig. 6 d displays the N, S-CDs fluorescence quenching with PA. As fluorescence quenching can be raised through aromatic nitrocompound percentages. The picric acid and other aromatic nitrocompounds quenching depends on the concentration-dependent and then established in Stern-Volmer quenching plots (Fig. 6e). The good linear response was observed with these Ksv plots for the aromatic nitro-compound. The Stern–Volmer quenching constant for aromatic nitro explosives was determined by Eq. (2) [30]:
(2) (I0/I) = 1 + Ksv [Q] →
where I0 – the initial PL intensities in lack of analyte, I – the PL intensities in linked concentrations of nitroaromatic compound and metal cation, Ksv - Stern–Volmer quenching constant, [Q] is the quencher concentration (mg L-1). The Io/I value indicates non-linear relationship for picric acid shown in Fig. 6e. The direct relationship of R2 = 0.965 was determined from linear range for PL response of 0–50 µM concentration for PA in N, S-CDs as displayed in Fig. 6f. The calculated Ksv values for PA is 4.4 × 104 M-1 (check these values). The detection limit (LOD) was calculated by slope method 3σ/k [30,31], where σ - standard deviation of blank run of N, S-CDs, k - slope from linear fit (regression coefficient). The limit of detection for PA is 0.07 µM. Table S1 displays the comparison of detection limits with earlier reported PA and sensor probes.

The N, S-CDs higher quantum yield (QY) provides a feasible type of promising fluorescence sensor probe for recognition of not only nitroexplosive compounds as well as also toxic metallic ions. To examine the N, S-CDs sensitivity for Fe3+ recognition, numerous concentrations of Fe3+ ion is addition to the solution of N, S-CDs as well as recorded the fluorescence intensities. Fig. 6a displays the PL spectral intensity of N, S-CDs in the existence of various percentages of Fe3+ ion. In addition, we observed that our as-prepared N, S-doped CDs solution in the non-existence of these metallic cations, displays strong PL emission at 451 nm as shown in Fig. 6a, which constantly declines by a rise in concentration of metallic cation. Fig 6b shows the mono-exponential fluorescent quenching of (Io/I) by Fe3+ ion. This designates N, S-CDs with metallic cation interaction ensuing for the quenched fluorescence intensities. The Stern-Volmer quenching constants (Ksv) for metal ions could be calculated by using the Stern-Volmer equation. The PL quenching efficacy displays good linear-response (R2 = 0.96) to Fe3+ ion as in range of 0–50 µM as displayed in Fig. 6c. The Ksv for Fe3+ ion is 3.6 × 104 M-1. The detection limit (LOD) was determined by slope method 3σ/m [32], as three times the blank measurement standard deviation with the non-existence of metallic ion, divided via the slope of standardization plot as well as metallic ion concentration. The calculated LOD for Fe3+ ion is 0.10 µM. Table S2 displays comparison of the detection limit with earlier reported Fe3+ ion and sensor probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for quenching of N, S-CDs – picric acid
ce:section.ce:para: We remarkably noted that nitrophenols should create more efficient emission quenching than other nitroaromatics. The quenching of fluorescence was normally ascribed to either an electron or energy transfer pathway. Meanwhile, aromatic nitro explosives are extremely electron-deficient, usually supposed to be capable of effectively quenched fluorescence via an electron-transfer pathway [33]. The efficacy of quenching is usually positive-correlation with the electron-withdrawing capability of nitroaromatic assemblies existing in aromatic nitro explosives, which could be in descending order of TNT > 2,4-DNT > 4-NT [34]. Nevertheless, a (TNT < 2,4-DNT < 4-NT) reverse tendency is noticed here. In common, energy band gap among the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of donor as well as acceptor is a chief strength inducer for electron transfer [35]. Hence, if electron transfer is a primary mechanism for quenching, then the nitroaromatic compound through low LUMO level could be the major quenching of fluorescence. We determined some of the energy levels of aromatic nitrocompounds via the density functional theory (DFT) technique by using the Becke, 3-parameter, Lee–Yang–Parr (B3LYP) functional with a 6-31+G* basis set of GAUSSIAN 09-software. However, the efficiency of fluorescence quenching of aromatic nitrocompounds are not match with energy band gaps (Table S3 and Fig. S2). All these outcomes advise that, fluorescent N, S-CDs quenching by aromatic nitro compounds not only merely described pronounced by the electron-transfer concept alone.

The feasible pathway for N, S-CDs fluorescence quenching by nitroexplosive PA is described by two-fold means like foster resonance energy transfer (FRET) as well as molecular interaction. Some rules are necessary to implement for the fluorescence quenching mechanism is owing to the FRET pathway. First, the quencher (PA - absorption spectra) could be fine overlapped with the fluorophore - emission spectra. Second, before and after the quencher addition, there was a variation in N,S-CDs PL lifetime. Energy transfer i.e., Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [36] is frequently believed to the leading quenching pathway at nanostructure surface.

The energy PL spectra from the donor (fluorophore) should be overlap with the energy UV-vis absorption spectra of the acceptor (i.e. quencher) is the main requisite for the FRET quenching pathway. In addition, the energy-transfer efficiency depends on the amount of overlap between the donor's PL spectra and the acceptor's absorption spectra [37]. Hence, our primary confirmation suggests a FRET pathway comprises an evaluation of the perceived quenching by spectral overlapping. Fig. 7 a demonstrates the picric acid's donor-acceptor spectral overlapping. As demonstrated, there is significant overlapping in PL spectra of N, S-CDs donor and UV-vis absorption of acceptor (PA - quencher). As common, nitrophenols have significant spectral overlap, whereas nitrotoluenes show a weaker overlap spectrum. As displayed in Fig. 7a, there was widespread overlap among the picric acid (absorption spectra) and N, S-CDs (emission spectra) which reveals that a decrease in PL intensity could be owing to FRET mechanism. Fig. 7a displays a strong relationship among overlap spectrum and observed N, S-CDs quenching. This association offers noble confirmation for an energy transfer quenching pathway.

In addition, for the confirmation of the FRET pathway, time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) to record the fluorescent lifetime decay of N, S-CDs in non-existence or existence of nitroaromatic explosive picric acid compound. Fig. 7b displays demonstrative outcomes when 0.05 mM nitroaromatic explosive picric acid was mixed by N, S-CDs. Fig. 7e shows the PL lifetime spectra of N, S-CDs and indicates the lifetime measurement of N, S-CDs with PA addition, which designates that there is variation in N, S-CDs PL lifetime (11.3 - 10.3 ns) is owing to dynamic quenching effect (i.e. change in average lifetime of fluorescence measurements earlier and later employment of analytes to the carbon dots), for the best understanding PL life time table was inserted in Table S5. These outcomes advised that fluorescence quenching is owing to the energy- transfer mechanism. In this instance, there was a decrease in fluorescent lifetime decay of picric acid-N, S-CDs was observed. Fig. 7e displays the diminish in fluorescent decay lifetime associated to the Stern−Volmer quenching constant (Ksv) which affords additional confirmation for the energy-transfer pathway. While summarizing all the evidence containing the association among the fluorescence quenching constant and the resultant integral overlap and fluorescent lifetime decay measurements designates that the N, S-CDs quenched fluorescence is mainly attributed to an energy-transfer pathway.

An additional cause for PL quenching could be owing to molecular interaction occurring among the N, S-CDs and picric acid is described by O & N-based assemblies existing on N, S-CDs interacts through the phenolic assembly of picric acid by the electrostatic interaction or else H-bonding is the outcome of quenched fluorescence [38]. Additionally, the presence of greater electron-withdrawing -NO2 assemblies on the phenyl ring could contact using N-based functional assemblies through electrostatic interaction which leads toward a decrease in PL intensity of N, S-CDs. The existence of –NO2 assemblies also contributes a significant part to quenched fluorescence [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for quenching of N, S-CDs - Fe3+ion
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, the pathway for fluorescence quenching to metallic cations was caused by two-fold pathways are inner-filtration effect (IFE), and photo-induced electron transfer (PET) [31]. Mainly, the UV absorption variation of N, S-CDs in the existence of Fe3+ ion studied. The UV-vis absorption spectra of Fe3+ with N, S-CDs in additional excitation spectra of N, S-CDs were finely overlap as well as re-absorbed over excitation area of N, S-doped CDs (Fig. 7a). The mean average fluorescence lifetime decay is nearly there is no variation from 11.3 to 11.1 ns which was shown (Fig. 7d) in existence of Fe3+ ion suggested as quenching of fluorescence is a static pathway as well as forms a ground state stable non-luminescent material. Therefore, these significances esteeming the N, S-CDs fluorescence quenching over the addition of precisely added N, S-CDs were appropriately followed inner-filteration effect [31].

The other feasible pathway for N, S-CDs quenched fluorescence was elucidated by stronger interaction occurs among Fe3+ ion as well as surface assemblies of N, S-CDs. The Fe3+ addition into N, S-doped CDs, it bound with the huge numeral of hydroxyl, and carboxylic acid, as well as amine assemblies employed on N, S-CDs surface as an outcome of chelation or else coordination makes electron transfers from the excited state of N, S-CDs to half-filled ‘d’ sub-shell which assists the non-emissive state of electron or recombination of hole-pair as a consequence of quenched fluorescence [40]. The graphical representation of fluorescence quenching of PA and Fe3+ is shown as Fig. 7c.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recognition of PA and Fe3+ in real world test samples
ce:section.ce:para: The recognition skill of N, S-CDs with lake water model (lake water taken from nearby location). The utility of examining the recoverability through analytical techniques for real-world uses of N, S-doped CDs for recognition of picric acid & Fe3+ ion. A varied concentration of about 10, 20, 30 µM were prepared. The cancer-causing metallic ions were made and then detected. The recovery percentage of the probes was determined for picric acid and Fe3+ ion displayed in Table S4. Hence, the acquired results study the feasibility of sensor probes in fluorometric recognition studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Filter paper-strip-based probe for visual recognition of toxins, anti-counterfeiting, and ink studies
ce:section.ce:para: The outstanding qualities of N, and S-CDs in means of stability, optical studies, and lower toxicity overlay the path for diverse studies. The amino acid-derived N, S-CDs were hired as anti-counterfeit studies like security applications through modern technologies. Therefore, we dissolved 0.005 g/mL of N, S-CDs filled with refilled-ink pen, after that we drew an array of digital designs like 888 numbers in filter paper-strip shown in Fig. 8 a. Subsequently, by using fluorescent inks made from N, S-CDs sketched various figures on filter paper and sketched secret figures are not detectable for bare eye, but under the UV-light irradiated at 365 nm, the sketched figures are clearly noticeable. Similarly fluorescent-ink studies were made earlier in paper-strip by sketch a doll and wrote a Tamil Poet Bharathiyar's slogan are evidently detectable in 365 nm under visible lamp Fig. 8 (b & c). Lastly, we signified that, the amino acid-derived N, S-CDs would be superior option for anti-theft studies with economical materials.

Using naked vision, we can detect hazardous environmental water toxins, for that we made up the fluorescent Whatman paper strip-based detection probe comprising N, S-CDs. For this detection, we could cut the Whatman filter paper (cellulose-based) into a preferred aspect. A part of the Whatman paper strip was drenched for 3 s in a newly made N, S-CDs sensor probe. A drenched paper strip comprising N, S-CDs was dried for 3 h at 50 °C. At various concentrations of different environmental water toxins were independently added to the fabricated N, S-CDs surface at the Whatman filter paper strip. The concentrations of Picric acid and Fe3+ ion were increased, i.e. separately added at 0, 20, 40, 60, 80 mM solutions were dried at room temperature. The dried paper strips was located at 365 nm under UV light irradiation. The acquired outcomes are displayed in Fig. 8(d & e) the fluorescent intensity from the filter paper strip slowly diminished, and the PL emission intensity was lastly quenched at 80 mM. These outcomes exposed that our as-prepared N, S-CDs did not recognize the environmental toxins only by using instrument-based practice, but we too detect by using optical resources (visible detection).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative study of N, S-CDs with previously reported sensor probes
ce:section.ce:para: We compared our proposed technique with previously reported works for PA and Fe3+ estimation which is shown in Tables S1 and S2. As we displayed in Tables S1 and S2, the previously stated techniques have higher recognition limits for picric acid and Fe3+ estimation. To the best of our knowledge, for the recognition of picric acid and Fe3+ ion there were no such kind of technique was stated with a very low limit of detection as reported. Henceforward, the obtained outcomes and studies validated that our as-prepared homocysteine-derived N, S-CDs might assist for the label-free recognition of picric acid and Fe3+ ion with a very better detection limit.

A well-dispersed water soluble, multi-functionalized N, S-doped CDs made from homocysteine and ethylene diamine through a facile microwave-assisted treatment method. The size and optical properties of as-prepared N, S-doped CDs were analyzed by HRTEM, XPS, FTIR, DLS, and Ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer and fluorescent measurements. As synthesized co-doped N, S-CDs, exhibit excitation wavelength-dependent emission, and the corresponding quantum yield was calculated to be around 45 %. PL-QY of our N, S-doped CDs is virtually not affected under UV exposure and long-term storage in solution state which creates them as a potential candidate for fluorescent sensor applications. The sensitive and selective recognition of Fe3+ ion and PA for N, S-CDs exhibits a lower detection limit, a prompt response, and an extensive linear range of detection. The complexation followed by dynamic electron transfer is a recognized possible mechanism for the witnessed sensing behavior for the quenching of N, S-doped CDs fluorescence on Fe3+ ion and picric acid addition. Furthermore, N, S-CDs also provide paper-based strip recognition, security information, and anti-counterfeiting studies. We believed our co-doped amino acid derived N, S-CDs exhibit a potentially cost-effective, sensitive and selective sensor for biological, and environmental applications.

Kumaresan Annamalai: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Ramya Ravichandran: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Conceptualization. Arun Annamalai: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Investigation. Anandhavalli Jeevarathinam: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Conceptualization. Ranganathan Suresh: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Sundaravadivel Elumalai: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge SRM Institute of Science and Technology for providing the major instrumentation facilities NRC-SRMIST and HRTEM Facility at SCIF set up with support from MNRE (Project No. 31/03/2014-15/PVSE-R&D, Government of India.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.materresbull.2024.113068.
